one_liner: A free open-source guide for coding Black hair in games.
search_doc:
A free open-source guide for coding Black hair in games.
Mechanisms: open-source-as-advocacy, representation-toolkit, brand-as-industry-resource, purpose-driven-product
Idea: Created in partnership with Open Source Afro Hair Library and Black 3D artists, the Code My Crown guide is a complete resource for the creation of authentic representations of Black hair in video games. This 100-page guide features 360-degree reference photography, step-by-step tutorials, and working files, all free for developers to download and use. By giving the gaming industry the tools to better represent the millions of Black Gamers who play their games, Code My Crown ensures that everyone sees their real beauty replicated with respect, care and accuracy.
Insight: Let Black gamers see their beauty accurately reflected in video games by helping Black creators set the standard for Black hair.

full_record.file_path: 2024/ENTERTAINMENT LIONS FOR GAMING/SILVER/Code My Crown - Dove (Edelman, London).webp

extracted.tagline: The world's first free, step-by-step guide to the creation and celebration of Black Hair within video games.
extracted.context: Millions of Black people play videogames. Despite this, the depiction of Black hair in gaming is often still inaccurate and highly stereotypical. In fact, 85% of Black Gamers feel that Black hair in sport is poorly represented in video games.
extracted.insight: Let Black gamers see their beauty accurately reflected in video games by helping Black creators set the standard for Black hair.
extracted.idea: Created in partnership with Open Source Afro Hair Library and Black 3D artists, the Code My Crown guide is a complete resource for the creation of authentic representations of Black hair in video games. This 100-page guide features 360-degree reference photography, step-by-step tutorials, and working files, all free for developers to download and use. By giving the gaming industry the tools to better represent the millions of Black Gamers who play their games, Code My Crown ensures that everyone sees their real beauty replicated with respect, care and accuracy.
execution: 100-page free downloadable guide to coding Black hair in video games
360-degree reference photography
Step-by-step tutorials and working files
Partnership with Open Source Afro Hair Library
Co-created with Black 3D artists
Distribution to game developers and studios
metrics: 85% of Black gamers now feel better represented
95% of Black gamers see the positive societal impact

inferred.one_liner: A free open-source guide for coding Black hair in games.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Dove turned a beauty brand position into an industry-wide development resource that addresses Black hair misrepresentation in video games; earned 1.81B+ impressions and reshaped the standard for in-game character creation.

visual.style_description: Wide editorial layout with a hero row of richly detailed Black female game avatars across the centre, the 'Code My Crown' title in script typography; left and right columns hold body copy, partner logos, press logos at the top and a results column on the right.

idea.rationale: Treating a 100-page open-source coding guide as the campaign asset — not a film, not an in-game skin — turns a beauty brand into a development tool for the gaming industry, which is an unexpected and durable creative move. The idea sits a little outside the entertainment-as-spectacle norm of the category, but the open-source-as-advocacy mechanic gives it real shelf life beyond a launch window.

strategy.rationale: Dove is Unilever's personal-care brand whose 20-year-old Real Beauty platform makes representation its core mission, so building the industry tool for accurate Black hair in games is precise mission-fit, not borrowed purpose. The insight — '85% of Black gamers feel Black hair is poorly represented in video games' — is a specific data point and matches a plain, observable industry failure that the work directly attacks. Partnering with the Open Source Afro Hair Library and Black 3D artists adds source-credibility to a claim that could otherwise read as outside-in.

execution.rationale: The deliverable is a 100-page free PDF guide with 360-degree reference photography, step-by-step tutorials and working files, distributed to game developers. That is the right format for the audience (developers, not players), but the orchestration on the board stops at distribution to studios — no in-game implementation case, no specific titles patched, no streamer or player-facing layer. For a Gaming Lions entry that's a narrow integration depth, which is the principal Execution constraint.

impact.rationale: '1.81B+ earned impressions' is a large entrant-reported reach claim and is the kind of unaudited number RIGOUR flags — '1.81B of what?' is not anchored. '+32% brand loyalty amongst Black community' is a brand-perception lift without an absolute base, and the '85% feel better represented' / '95% see positive societal impact' figures are claimed but not tied to a sample size disclosed on the board. The category asks for player-engagement metrics (downloads, studio adoptions, characters built using the guide) and none are surfaced, which is the principal soft spot.

why_it_won.verdict: Silver because the open-source guide is an unusually durable mission-fit for Dove, but the case stops at developer distribution and entrant-reported reach numbers rather than evidence of adoption inside actual games.

one_liner: A pop anthem and a painted church become the game's launch.
search_doc:
A pop anthem and a painted church become the game's launch.
Mechanisms: celebrity-as-prop, fine-art-collaboration, music-as-game-launch, cultural-symbol-subversion
Idea: Seduce new fans by leaning into the Dark Gothic style of the game by painting Diablo's demonic imagery on the ceiling of a 16th century church and ignite culture by pairing it with a global pop icon and fan of the brand, Halsey (3.2x affinity) and her good friend and fellow Diablo fan, SUGA from BTS (2.9x affinity).

full_record.file_path: 2024/ENTERTAINMENT LIONS FOR GAMING/BRONZE/Diablo Anthem - Diablo (72Andsunny Los Angeles).jpg

extracted.tagline: Halsey ft Suga of BTS — Lilith — Diablo IV Anthem
extracted.idea: Seduce new fans by leaning into the Dark Gothic style of the game by painting Diablo's demonic imagery on the ceiling of a 16th century church and ignite culture by pairing it with a global pop icon and fan of the brand, Halsey (3.2x affinity) and her good friend and fellow Diablo fan, SUGA from BTS (2.9x affinity).
execution: The Church: Famed Baroque artist Adam Miller and team used 2,400 sq. feet of canvas to cover the 120-ft high ceiling in Diablo IV demons — including the Daughter of Hatred herself, Lilith. Took over a month to complete and served as the backdrop for Halsey's music video.

The Music Video: Partnered with Halsey to remake her track 'Lilith' — also the name of the in-game villain. Directed by Henry Hobson; drove 20 million views, 600K+ tweets within 24 hours, hit #1 on iTunes in 63 countries, #4 on Spotify, trended #1 on YouTube.
Collaboration track with Halsey featuring Suga of BTS
metrics: Unaided awareness jumped over 300% from 4.3% to 18.7%
20 million views on the music video

inferred.context: Diablo IV needed to recruit new fans beyond its core dark-fantasy gamer base for one of the biggest game launches of the year.
inferred.one_liner: A pop anthem and a painted church become the game's launch.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Recast a video game launch as a pop-culture moment by commissioning a Halsey-Suga track and a Baroque church-ceiling artwork; drove $666M in sales over five days, 300%+ unaided awareness lift, and chart-topping music performance.

visual.style_description: Dark Gothic case board: a brooding portrait of Lilith descending a stone aisle anchors the centre under the Diablo IV logo; red metric callouts flank the hero on a near-black backdrop; three captioned sub-images at the bottom break out the idea, the church installation, and the music video.

idea.rationale: Pairing a Halsey-Suga collaboration with a Baroque-style ceiling painting of Diablo's Lilith in a 16th-century church is a polished culture push, but at its core it is the familiar 'celebrity music video as game launch' play with a fine-art stunt bolted on. The church ceiling is a distinctive craft moment; the underlying mechanic is celebrity-as-prop rather than a category-redefining gaming idea.

strategy.rationale: Diablo is Blizzard's three-decade-old dark-fantasy ARPG franchise whose identity is gothic horror and demonology, so commissioning a Baroque demon-ceiling and a song named after the in-game villain is coherent with the brand's mission rather than borrowed purpose. The strategic case for using Halsey and Suga rests on the '3.2x affinity' and '2.9x affinity' figures cited on the board — these are entrant-reported audience-affinity claims with no source or comparison base, which is the principal Strategy soft spot. The brand-fit is solid; the data backing the talent choice is thin.

execution.rationale: The orchestration is a two-asset launch — a painted church ceiling backdrop and a Halsey-Suga music video directed by Henry Hobson — released into music platforms (iTunes, Spotify, YouTube) rather than into gaming surfaces. There is no streamer integration, no in-game tie-in, no esports moment, no creator partnership layer described on the board: it is a film and music campaign that happens to launch a game. For a Gaming Lions entry the native-to-category integration depth is shallow.

impact.rationale: '$666 million revenue in just 5 days' and 'over 10 million copies sold' are real, absolute business outcomes anchored in publicly tracked game-launch figures — the strongest part of the case. 'Unaided awareness jumped 300% from 4.3% to 18.7%' is properly anchored with both base and lift. However '2B+ impressions', '600k+ tweets', '20M music-video views' and '#1 on iTunes in 63 countries' are press / vanity metrics, and Diablo IV's launch would have hit those numbers with or without the church-and-anthem — attribution of the sales outcome to this specific campaign is not isolated.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the celebrity-anthem-plus-church-painting is polished and on-brand for Diablo, but the work lives outside the gaming experience itself and the headline sales numbers belong to the launch, not provably to this campaign.

one_liner: Your in-game skin IS your loan collateral.
search_doc:
Your in-game skin IS your loan collateral.
Mechanisms: in-game-asset-as-collateral, gaming-economy-financialisation, audience-redefinition, product-as-message
Idea: A loan product where the bank accepts in-game assets (skins, accounts, items in Clash of Clans, Pokemon Go, FIFA, Minecraft) as valid collateral, unlocking credit for young gamers.
Insight: Gamers own valuable in-game assets and digital goods (skins, characters, accounts) which they treat as real-world value but which banks do not recognise as collateral.

full_record.file_path: 2024/ENTERTAINMENT LIONS FOR GAMING/GOLD/Gamer Loan - Banco Del Pacifico (Paradais DDB, Guayaquil).jpeg

extracted.tagline: Changing the way gamers experience banking.
extracted.context: 7 out of 10 young Ecuadorians can access a loan because they're unable to afford what banks require as collateral.
extracted.insight: Gamers own valuable in-game assets and digital goods (skins, characters, accounts) which they treat as real-world value but which banks do not recognise as collateral.
extracted.idea: A loan product where the bank accepts in-game assets (skins, accounts, items in Clash of Clans, Pokemon Go, FIFA, Minecraft) as valid collateral, unlocking credit for young gamers.
execution: In-game assets from Clash of Clans, Pokemon Go, FIFA and Minecraft accepted as loan collateral
Collateral examples shown with USD valuations ($1,250 / $1,021 / $1,500)
Pre-approved loans up to $5,321 USD
Validation and verification process for gaming accounts as collateral
metrics: 7 out of 10 young Ecuadorians can access a loan
$282,956 pre-approved in first week of campaign

inferred.one_liner: Your in-game skin IS your loan collateral.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reframes gaming inventories as legitimate financial assets, opening bank credit to young Ecuadorians previously excluded by traditional collateral requirements; drove a 248% lift in loan requests.

visual.style_description: Neon-lit hero portrait of a young gamer in a hoodie and headset gripping a controller whose screen reads '$5,321 USD', framed by purple-pink gradient lighting; top row shows four in-game collateral cards (Clash of Clans, Pokemon Go, FIFA, Minecraft) with dollar valuations; problem/opportunity/solution text columns left and right.

idea.rationale: Accepting Clash of Clans accounts, Pokemon Go inventories, FIFA squads and Minecraft items as bank-grade collateral is a hard-product reframe rather than a content idea — the bank's underwriting rules are the creative work. It rewrites who counts as creditworthy in a country where most young people are locked out of credit, which is far more original than the usual gaming-skin merchandising play.

strategy.rationale: Banco del Pacífico is one of Ecuador's largest private banks with a long-running positioning around financial inclusion for young Ecuadorians, so treating a generation's most valuable digital assets as legitimate collateral is exact mission-fit, not borrowed purpose. The insight that '7 out of 10 young Ecuadorians can't access a loan because of collateral requirements' is a specific, locally credible data point that frames the product as a real-world remedy rather than a stunt. The link from local youth-exclusion-from-credit to gaming assets as a recognised store of value is a clean, defensible argument.

execution.rationale: Execution is the underwriting layer itself: validation and verification of gaming accounts as collateral, transparent USD valuations on individual asset types ($1,250 / $1,021 / $1,500), and pre-approved credit lines up to $5,321 USD distributed through the bank's own channels. The product mechanics are the campaign, which is why it reads as 'product-as-message' rather than a paid ad, but the lack of streamer or in-game distribution layers limits the orchestration story for a category whose richest winners run across multiple gaming surfaces.

impact.rationale: '+248% increase in loan requests' is the right business outcome for a credit product and is the player-engagement-adjacent metric the category asks for, though '248% of what base?' is not disclosed on the board. '$282,956 pre-approved in the first week' is an absolute monetary figure, which anchors the percentage. Press coverage is not shown, so the case rests on first-party bank numbers, but those numbers are the ones a bank's CFO actually cares about.

why_it_won.verdict: Gold because the bank rewrites its own collateral policy to recognise gaming inventories as real wealth — the product is the message, and it delivers a credible credit-access outcome in a country where 70% of young people can't get a loan.

one_liner: Lego brick-building becomes a Fortnite survival mode.
search_doc:
Lego brick-building becomes a Fortnite survival mode.
Mechanisms: branded-game-mode, cross-platform-partnership, kid-safe-metaverse, ip-co-production
Idea: To address what our audiences desired within the gaming space, a long-term partnership between Epic Games and the Lego Group was struck — with a core mission to develop fun and safe digital spaces for children and families. The partnership's first launch was Lego Fortnite, a survival crafting game, which brings the magic of Lego building into the Fortnite ecosystem.
Insight: We needed to introduce a new way of engaging kids and families, significant enough to get their attention and offer them an immersive, intuitive way to experience the Lego System in play and its endless creative possibilities. How do we make the joy of building more accessible to a wide range of digital-first audiences in a safe way?

full_record.file_path: 2024/ENTERTAINMENT LIONS FOR GAMING/BRONZE/Lego Fortnite - Lego group (Lego Group Billund).jpg

extracted.tagline: Lego play meets the Fortnite universe in an all-new survival crafting adventure.
extracted.context: The Lego Group's mission has always been to 'inspire and develop the builders of tomorrow.' However, it has been increasingly difficult to reach younger digital-native audiences given the vast amount of entertainment options and platforms around them, many of which are not fully safe for children.
extracted.insight: We needed to introduce a new way of engaging kids and families, significant enough to get their attention and offer them an immersive, intuitive way to experience the Lego System in play and its endless creative possibilities. How do we make the joy of building more accessible to a wide range of digital-first audiences in a safe way?
extracted.idea: To address what our audiences desired within the gaming space, a long-term partnership between Epic Games and the Lego Group was struck — with a core mission to develop fun and safe digital spaces for children and families. The partnership's first launch was Lego Fortnite, a survival crafting game, which brings the magic of Lego building into the Fortnite ecosystem.
execution: Launch of Lego Fortnite survival crafting game inside the Fortnite universe
Lego System minifigure-style avatars in the Fortnite world
Building, crafting and exploration gameplay with Lego logic
Safe digital space designed for kids and families
metrics: Highest search for 'Lego' in the past 5 years
8.3M views on first month of launch at Twitch

inferred.one_liner: Lego brick-building becomes a Fortnite survival mode.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Repositions Lego inside the world's largest gaming platform via a co-built survival mode, driving 8M Lego accounts, #1 YouTube Gaming trend status, and 2.5B earned media impressions.

visual.style_description: Bright photographic hero of a crowd of Lego minifigures dressed as Fortnite characters in front of a blue sky, with the campaign title set in white over a navy stripe; the lower half is a structured 3-column case-board layout with challenge/idea text, in-game screenshots, and stacked metrics plus a press-logo grid on the right.

idea.rationale: A long-term partnership that places a survival-crafting Lego mode inside Fortnite is a major business deal more than it is a creative idea — the creative work is the brand IP-onto-platform move itself. It scores on scale and native-to-category fit (it IS a real playable mode, not a logo skin), but the underlying mechanic — branded-game-mode via IP co-production — is a familiar Fortnite pattern rather than a category-redefining reframe.

strategy.rationale: The Lego Group is the Danish family-owned toymaker whose 'inspire and develop the builders of tomorrow' mission has anchored its identity for decades, so building inside Fortnite is exact mission-fit — the partnership's stated goal is 'safe digital spaces for children and families', which is the brand's literal positioning. The insight that Lego needed a new way to reach digital-native audiences inside platforms where many existing options are not child-safe is a plain, observable truth backed by the broader industry shift to gaming, though the board frames it as a strategic statement rather than a sourced data point. Mission-fit is strong; insight is intuitive rather than evidenced.

execution.rationale: Execution is a co-built survival-crafting mode inside the Fortnite ecosystem with Lego-style minifigure avatars and Lego-logic building mechanics. The board describes the product but not the marketing orchestration around it: no creator roster, no launch-phase structure, no in-platform amplification mechanic, no out-of-game press loop is detailed. For a Cannes Gaming entry the case rests heavily on the business deal itself rather than on a layered creative execution.

impact.rationale: '8M Lego accounts created' is a real player-engagement number — the category's preferred metric — and the right outcome for the brand's goal of reaching digital-native audiences. '#1 trending video on YouTube Gaming', '8.3M views on Twitch in the first month' and 'highest search for Lego in the past 5 years' are credible directional signals of cultural traction. '2.5B earned media impressions' is the kind of entrant-reported reach figure RIGOUR flags as unaudited — '2.5B of what?' is not disclosed — and the case offers no commercial outcome (toy sales lift, subscription revenue) that ties the player numbers to Lego's actual business.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the Lego-Fortnite mode is precisely mission-fit and delivers a real 8M-account engagement number, but the work is a business partnership more than a creative campaign and the case for it is thin on orchestration.

campaign: Play Has No Limits feat. King Gnu
one_liner: King Gnu becomes PlayStation's iconic game characters.
search_doc:
King Gnu becomes PlayStation's iconic game characters.
Mechanisms: music-video-as-brand-platform, celebrity-as-game-character, gaming-culture-tribute, cross-platform-cultural-collab
Idea: Turn PlayStation's 'Play Has No Limits' slogan into a music video where the band King Gnu transforms into four iconic PlayStation characters across cinematic in-game worlds.
Insight: PlayStation fans and music fans converge in Japan; embodying iconic game characters through a beloved local band turns a brand slogan into a shared cultural performance.

full_record.file_path: 2024/ENTERTAINMENT LIONS FOR GAMING/BRONZE/Play Has No Limit Feat. King Gnu - Playstation (Six Inc, Tokyo).jpg

extracted.campaign: Play Has No Limits feat. King Gnu
execution: Music video collaboration between PlayStation and Japanese band King Gnu
Live-action band members styled as four iconic PlayStation game characters
Cinematic vignettes recreating worlds from PlayStation game titles
Multi-platform rollout across YouTube, X, TikTok with extensive campaign assets

inferred.insight: PlayStation fans and music fans converge in Japan; embodying iconic game characters through a beloved local band turns a brand slogan into a shared cultural performance.
inferred.idea: Turn PlayStation's 'Play Has No Limits' slogan into a music video where the band King Gnu transforms into four iconic PlayStation characters across cinematic in-game worlds.
inferred.one_liner: King Gnu becomes PlayStation's iconic game characters.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Collaboration with Japanese band King Gnu turns PlayStation's tagline into a music-video tribute to its iconic characters, generating 16M video views and 145M campaign social reach with broad music-press pickup in Japan.

visual.style_description: Top row shows four cinematic portraits of King Gnu band members costumed as PlayStation game characters around a central PlayStation crown and 'Play Has No Limits' lockup; press pull-quotes underneath, a strip of dark cinematic game-world stills in the middle, and a metrics + logo block at the bottom.

idea.rationale: Casting the members of King Gnu as four iconic PlayStation characters in a cinematic music video is a tidy execution of the 'Play Has No Limits' tagline, but it is fundamentally a celebrity-music-video format applied to a brand platform. The character-transformation conceit is well-crafted and culturally Japanese-specific, but it does not reframe the brand-gaming relationship — it dramatises an existing tagline.

strategy.rationale: PlayStation is Sony's global gaming platform whose 'Play Has No Limits' positioning has been used since the PS5 launch to argue that play transcends genre and medium — pairing it with King Gnu, a major Japanese band, is coherent with that mission rather than borrowed purpose. The local-market fit (King Gnu is one of Japan's most-streamed acts; PlayStation is a Japanese-owned brand) gives the partnership a credible cultural-precision rationale, though the board provides no quantified audience or insight data to back the choice of band. The mission-to-execution line is clean; the insight backing is implicit.

execution.rationale: Execution is a music video with four cinematic vignettes recreating PlayStation game worlds and the band styled as iconic characters, rolled out across YouTube, X and TikTok with multi-asset variants. The orchestration is single-asset content amplified through music and entertainment press (Billboard, Tower Records, Natalie, Real Sound, Fashion Press, Yahoo), which is correct for a music-collaboration campaign but light on in-game, streamer or PlayStation-platform integrations that would deepen the gaming-native claim.

impact.rationale: '16M video views' is a concrete, anchored video metric and the right outcome for a music-video-as-campaign. '145M campaign social reach' is an entrant-reported reach figure with no methodology — '145M of which platforms?' is not disclosed — and '1952K engagements' is a directional but unbenchmarked figure. No PlayStation-platform business outcome (console sales, PlayStation Network sign-ups, attach-rate for any specific title) is named, which the category typically expects from a platform-brand campaign.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the King Gnu transformation is a culturally precise Japanese music-video for the PlayStation tagline, but the case rests on a single music asset and surface-reach metrics without any platform business outcome.

one_liner: Players become CCTV detectives inside the game's own park.
search_doc:
Players become CCTV detectives inside the game's own park.
Mechanisms: in-game-lore-experience, interactive-scavenger-hunt, generative-animation-storytelling, community-engagement-stunt
Idea: The project needed to build the game's lore, while inspiring fans to develop and share their theories and content, thus testing the project's impact and visibility.
Insight: Mobile gamers crave deeper lore and active participation in their favourite worlds — a passive update couldn't celebrate five years of Brawl Stars.

full_record.file_path: 2024/ENTERTAINMENT LIONS FOR GAMING/BRONZE/Star Park CCTV - Brawl Stars (Supercell, Helsinki).webp

extracted.tagline: A first of its kind origin story experience.
extracted.context: Brawl Stars is one of the world's most popular mobile shooter games. It features Brawlers fighting inside an abandoned amusement park called Starr Park. For the 5th anniversary of the game, we wanted to re-ignite engagement within the player community by using only in-game and social channels.
extracted.idea: The project needed to build the game's lore, while inspiring fans to develop and share their theories and content, thus testing the project's impact and visibility.
execution: Created a six week long interactive story scavenger hunt drove fans to an in-game event — Starr Park CCTV
Fans pieced together the story of Starr Park by finding clues hidden within a fully functioning security room with 64 cameras
Built using Unreal Engine, simulating more than six weeks of generative animation
Combined more than 20 thousand character iterations of park patrons
Fans could monitor the park through five screens streaming from 64 cameras placed around the park
Daily journal entries from a fictitious security guard
Voicemails made from park patrons to our emergency call center
Time-lines on social posts that fans could use to get early access to animated story drops

inferred.insight: Mobile gamers crave deeper lore and active participation in their favourite worlds — a passive update couldn't celebrate five years of Brawl Stars.
inferred.one_liner: Players become CCTV detectives inside the game's own park.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Transformed Brawl Stars' fifth anniversary into a six-week CCTV-style detective experience inside the game's universe; drew 89M visits, 130M+ collective views and 8.5K+ player videos with zero media spend.

visual.style_description: Wall of TV-style CCTV thumbnails tiles the entire background; the central hero is a vintage security-monitor mock-up showing in-game footage, with the glitch-styled '#StarrParkCCTV' title above; bottom strip lists the headline metrics in bold neon callouts.

idea.rationale: Building a CCTV-style scavenger hunt across 64 fictional security cameras inside the game's own amusement park is a smart in-game-lore mechanic that turns passive players into detectives across six weeks. The format is genuinely native to the title's universe (Starr Park is a canonical Brawl Stars location) rather than a bolted-on ad, which earns the gameplay-as-the-message claim.

strategy.rationale: Brawl Stars is Supercell's mobile shooter — one of the world's most-played mobile games — with a dedicated player community that values lore and ARG-style mysteries; building a fifth-anniversary experience that demands fans piece together the park's backstory is precise mission-fit, not borrowed purpose. The insight that the community wants deeper participation in the game's mythology is intuitive and supported by the eventual UGC volume, though the board does not cite pre-campaign community-research data. The strategic choice to spend $0 on paid media because the community is the channel is coherent with the game's organic-growth track record.

execution.rationale: Execution is an interactive multi-layer ARG: a six-week Unreal Engine simulation with 64 streamed CCTV cameras, daily journal entries from a fictional security guard, voicemails to an in-fiction emergency line, and timed social drops feeding back into the experience. Orchestration is in-game and social-only with no paid amplification, which is correct for the player community but limits the work's cross-cultural reach; the absence of any press pickup on the board reinforces that this lived inside its niche.

impact.rationale: '89M visits to the experience' and '8,500+ user-generated videos' with '130M+ collective views' are real player-engagement metrics — exactly what the category asks for — and the '$0 media spend' anchor makes the earned nature of the conversation credible. No business outcome (player retention lift, daily active users, in-game purchase impact on the anniversary) is reported, which is the principal soft spot: a five-year mobile-game anniversary should ultimately move the retention or revenue needle, and none of those numbers are surfaced on the board.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the in-game CCTV scavenger hunt is genuinely native to Brawl Stars' lore and earns real player participation, but the case stops at engagement volume without showing the retention or revenue lift a fifth anniversary needs to justify.

one_liner: The video game tactician becomes the real football team's tactician.
search_doc:
The video game tactician becomes the real football team's tactician.
Mechanisms: gameplay-as-audition, fan-as-talent, simulation-meets-reality, branded-recruitment-stunt
Idea: For the launch of Football Manager 2024, Xbox helped the National League tap into a new pool of talent: gamers. Football Manager 24 uses live data to replicate the real game, testing tactics from past games and current results. Xbox turned the gameplay into a recruitment tool to find a top gaming talent as the new tactician for professional team Bromley FC. The football and gaming community went wild for the opportunity, with views on the job ad, and one gamer got the job. The tactician used game data to advise the club in real life, helping Bromley FC achieve their highest league position ever and reveal defeats in the league. Our documentary changed perceptions of how football talent is recruited and showed how Xbox powers your dreams.
Insight: No football fan is alone in thinking they could manage a team. Many great hundreds of hours a month engaging in football.

full_record.file_path: 2024/ENTERTAINMENT LIONS FOR GAMING/GRAND PRIX/The Everyday Tactician - XBOX (McCann, London).jpg

extracted.tagline: Blurring the lines between simulation and the real world.
extracted.context: Football is flourishing in the Premier League, the richest league in the world. They have the best talent, and data, which is transforming the sport. But four leagues below, in the National League, teams are struggling and being left behind.
extracted.insight: No football fan is alone in thinking they could manage a team. Many great hundreds of hours a month engaging in football.
extracted.idea: For the launch of Football Manager 2024, Xbox helped the National League tap into a new pool of talent: gamers. Football Manager 24 uses live data to replicate the real game, testing tactics from past games and current results. Xbox turned the gameplay into a recruitment tool to find a top gaming talent as the new tactician for professional team Bromley FC. The football and gaming community went wild for the opportunity, with views on the job ad, and one gamer got the job. The tactician used game data to advise the club in real life, helping Bromley FC achieve their highest league position ever and reveal defeats in the league. Our documentary changed perceptions of how football talent is recruited and showed how Xbox powers your dreams.
execution: Live broadcast tactical recruitment campaign for Bromley FC
Gameplay used as audition tool to find a real-world tactician
Selected gamer joined Bromley FC coaching staff
Documentary content tracking the gamer's impact on the club
Press partnerships with major sports and entertainment outlets
metrics: 190% increase in FM gamers on Xbox
Best season in Bromley's 130 year history

inferred.one_liner: The video game tactician becomes the real football team's tactician.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned Football Manager gameplay into a real-world recruitment pipeline for a struggling football club; delivered 1.5B impressions, a 190% lift in FM gamers on Xbox, and Bromley FC's best season in 130 years.

visual.style_description: Hero portrait of the chosen gamer-tactician seated pitch-side in a stadium, framed by quote callouts and a large red metric column; bottom strip shows the BT Sport documentary thumbnails and a row of press logos.

idea.rationale: Turning Football Manager gameplay into a literal audition for a real coaching role at a real club collapses the wall between simulation and the professional sport in a way no prior gaming activation has done. It is native to the title (the game's whole proposition IS tactical recruitment) and culturally unmistakable, which is the rarest combination in branded gaming.

strategy.rationale: Xbox is Microsoft's gaming platform whose 'Power Your Dreams' positioning argues that gaming has real-world value beyond entertainment, making a literal career-pipeline-into-football a precise mission-fit rather than borrowed purpose. The insight that football fans believe they could manage a team — and the data point that they spend hundreds of hours simulating it — is a plain, observable cultural truth that the work weaponises directly. The framing of the National League as data-poor versus the Premier League is a credible sport-economy observation that grounds the case for gamer-as-tactician.

execution.rationale: The orchestration runs from a live tactical recruitment open call, through gameplay-as-audition, into an actual coaching-staff placement at Bromley FC and a documentary tracking the on-pitch impact across a full season. Broadcast and sports-press partnerships (BBC, Match of the Day, BT Sport, The Athletic, FourFourTwo) layered the gaming press (Hypebeast, LADbible, GOAL) onto mainstream sport pickup, which is the right channel mix for a title that lives at the intersection of football and gaming.

impact.rationale: The most load-bearing number is the player-engagement metric the category asks for — '190% increase in FM gamers on Xbox' and 'most played FM ever' — which are exactly the business outcomes the platform cares about. The 1.5B impressions figure is entrant-reported, but the breadth of unpaid press across sport and gaming verticals makes the conversation credible, and 'Bromley FC's best season in 130 years' is a verifiable real-world business outcome anchored in a publicly tracked league table rather than a brand metric.

why_it_won.verdict: Wins the Grand Prix because it dissolves the boundary between video-game simulation and professional sport: a gamer chosen through gameplay actually coaches a real team to its best season in 130 years.

one_liner: The PC cooler becomes the beer fridge.
search_doc:
The PC cooler becomes the beer fridge.
Mechanisms: product-as-message, gaming-culture-tribute, pain-point-hardware, branded-hardware-stunt
Idea: Heineken built a real high-performance PC whose cooling system doubles as a Heineken fridge — a single piece of hardware that keeps the rig cool and the beer cold.
Insight: Gamers obsess about cooling their PC to gain performance — the same cooling system can also keep beer cold, turning a pain point of the setup into a brand opportunity.

full_record.file_path: 2024/ENTERTAINMENT LIONS FOR GAMING/SILVER/The Gaming Fridge- Heineken (LePub, Milan _ LePub, Sao Paulo).jpg

extracted.tagline: The ultimate gaming PC that is also a Heineken fridge.
extracted.context: Gen Z and Millennial gamers think hot PCs and warm beers are a problem. Heineken saw that a hotter machine means a hotter beer, so the brand chose to solve the cooling problem at the source.
extracted.insight: Gamers obsess about cooling their PC to gain performance — the same cooling system can also keep beer cold, turning a pain point of the setup into a brand opportunity.
extracted.idea: Heineken built a real high-performance PC whose cooling system doubles as a Heineken fridge — a single piece of hardware that keeps the rig cool and the beer cold.
execution: Real working prototype of the Gaming Fridge PC
Reveal launched on David Hartford / influencer channel
Streamers and gaming community spread the news
Even the biggest e-sports streamer in the world raved about it
Trophy awarded at a League of Legends event
metrics: Highest brand power and differentiation with Heineken in history

inferred.one_liner: The PC cooler becomes the beer fridge.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turns a gamer pain point (hot rig, warm beer) into branded hardware, generating 340M gamer impressions and $25M in earned media and setting a Heineken record for brand power and differentiation.

visual.style_description: Dark cyberpunk hero shot of an illuminated green Heineken fridge integrated into a gaming PC tower, glowing neon-green logo and tagline in pixel-font 'GAMING_FR1DG3'; right side stacks bold metrics on black; bottom strip carries a row of streamer reaction stills and a wall of press/media logos.

idea.rationale: Engineering a single piece of hardware whose PC-cooling system doubles as a beer fridge is a clean pain-point-hardware idea that fuses Heineken's product with gaming-rig culture in a way that reads instantly. It scores high on memorability and on native-to-category fit — gamers obsess about cooling, and the joke is the engineering — though the underlying mechanic is a single-stunt object rather than a category-redefining reframe.

strategy.rationale: Heineken is the global premium-lager brand whose long-running positioning ties the product to social moments — historically sport, increasingly gaming — so building a piece of native gaming hardware is coherent with the brand's ongoing push to be 'the beer of gamers' rather than borrowed purpose. The insight that gamers consider hot PCs and warm beer twin problems is plausible and observably true in PC-gaming culture, though the board does not back it with a specific data point. Mission-fit is solid; insight-veracity is intuitive rather than evidenced.

execution.rationale: A real working prototype is the asset, revealed through an established gaming-tech influencer (David Hartford) and amplified by streamers including a top esports name, with a trophy moment at a League of Legends event. The orchestration is single-asset PR — one object, lots of streamer pickup, broad gaming-press coverage (IGN, Kotaku, PC Gamer, Gamesradar, Hypebeast) — which is right for a stunt-product but lacks the multi-phase, multi-surface depth of higher-tier Gaming work.

impact.rationale: '340M gamers impacted' is an entrant-reported reach claim with no methodology disclosed — '340M of what audience?' is not anchored — and '$25M earned media' is an AVE-style figure that is unaudited. 'Highest brand power and differentiation with Heineken in history' is a brand-tracker claim without the absolute scores, which limits jury-credibility. The press wall (Bloomberg, Yahoo, VICE, AdAge, IGN, Kotaku, PC Gamer) is broad and credible as a signal of earned conversation, but no player-engagement metric (pre-orders, units made, build hours streamed) is named, which the category specifically asks for.

why_it_won.verdict: Silver because the Gaming Fridge is a tight, native pain-point object that wins on memorability and gaming-press resonance, but the case relies on entrant-reported reach and an AVE rather than a measured product or brand outcome.

campaign: Doritos Silent — The World's First AI Augmented Snack
one_liner: AI silences the Doritos crunch for gamers.
search_doc:
AI silences the Doritos crunch for gamers.
Mechanisms: ai-as-product-feature, product-flaw-into-feature, gaming-culture-tribute, branded-software-utility
Idea: Launch a new product, Doritos Silent, the world's first AI augmented snack — a powerful, crowd-pleasing product — the name held Doritos crunch, now with no sound.
Insight: 85% of gamers love to snack on Doritos while gaming. 2/3 find others crunching while gaming annoying. #1 complaint in voice chat (Doritos not named, but they came).

full_record.file_path: 2024/ENTERTAINMENT LIONS FOR GAMING/SILVER/The World_s First AI Augmented Snack - Doritos Pepsico (Pepsico, Reading _ Sips & Bites, London).jpg

extracted.campaign: Doritos Silent — The World's First AI Augmented Snack
extracted.tagline: By silencing the crunch, we made it even louder.
extracted.context: Gamers love Doritos, but snacking and gaming are not a match made in heaven. Owning a huge bandwagon community but facing the iconic crunch can cause irritation, distraction and, even worse, deaths.
extracted.insight: 85% of gamers love to snack on Doritos while gaming. 2/3 find others crunching while gaming annoying. #1 complaint in voice chat (Doritos not named, but they came).
extracted.idea: Launch a new product, Doritos Silent, the world's first AI augmented snack — a powerful, crowd-pleasing product — the name held Doritos crunch, now with no sound.
execution: Doritos Silent: an AI-powered crunch cancellation technology
Free downloadable software for gamers that detects and silences the Doritos crunch in real time
Influencer demos with streamers using the technology live on stream
Distinct branded packaging — black Doritos Silent bag

inferred.one_liner: AI silences the Doritos crunch for gamers.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Doritos turns its loudest brand asset — the crunch — into a silenceable AI feature for gamers, generating 3.2BN reach, 76% brand uplift and an additional $50M sales growth on the back of broad gaming and mainstream press coverage.

visual.style_description: Hero photograph of a person's ear filled with a Doritos chip used as an earbud-style piece, set on a warm dark backdrop with red metric labels and pull-quotes; left column lists insight stats, right column features press headline quotes, with a row of metrics and press logos across the bottom and a product shot of the black Doritos Silent bag on the lower right.

idea.rationale: Inverting Doritos' most famous brand asset — the loud crunch — into a silenceable software feature for gamers is a clean product-flaw-into-feature move that lands the tagline 'by silencing the crunch, we made it even louder' instantly. The category claim 'world's first AI augmented snack' is a borderline superlative, but the underlying mechanic (real-time AI cancellation of one branded sound in voice chat) is a tangible technical artefact, not just a stunt label.

strategy.rationale: Doritos is PepsiCo's flagship tortilla-chip brand whose long-standing identity has been the deliberate disruption of the crunch — so reframing that liability inside gamers' voice chat is exact brand-fit, not borrowed purpose. The board cites two specific insight points — '85% of gamers love to snack on Doritos while gaming' and '2/3 find others crunching while gaming annoying' — which anchor the problem in a real friction (the #1 voice-chat complaint) rather than a generic 'we love gamers' positioning. Both numbers are entrant-reported with no source, which is the only soft spot.

execution.rationale: Execution layers a real downloadable AI noise-cancellation utility, a distinct black 'Doritos Silent' SKU and streamer demos using the tool live on stream — three connected surfaces (software, packaged product, creator content) that match the audience's actual rituals. The orchestration is thinner than the Grand Prix benchmark — no specific streamer roster or in-game integration phases on the board — but the artefacts named are the right ones for a PC-gaming snacking moment.

impact.rationale: '+$50M sales growth' is an absolute monetary business outcome, exactly the figure a PepsiCo brand cares about, which anchors the case. '+76% brand uplift' and '+1.8M website visits' are useful directional signals, and '3.2BN reach' is the kind of entrant-reported figure RIGOUR flags as unaudited — '3.2BN of what impressions?' is not disclosed. No software-download count is given, which is the player-engagement metric the category typically expects for an AI utility.

why_it_won.verdict: Silver because the crunch-cancellation idea is precisely brand-native and backed by a real $50M sales outcome, but the case leans on unanchored reach claims and never reports how many gamers actually installed the tool.

one_liner: Reality filmed like a video game to reach recruited gamers.
search_doc:
Reality filmed like a video game to reach recruited gamers.
Mechanisms: video-game-aesthetic-hijack, purpose-driven-casting, gaming-culture-tribute, cinematic-craft-as-message
Idea: 'This is Not a Game' was an unconventional project that demanded doing everything differently, even from the casting stage. Finding talent who could move and feel like game characters was not easy. The casting took place in schools, acting houses, gaming tournaments, and even video game fan pages. It was important to understand the world of video games to get into character immediately, so all of them were gamers.
Insight: Movistar Telecom is a brand that is committed to the digital environment. It cares about providing gamers with a safe space. 40% of Movistar Telecom business is in gaming, and the responsibility to address an issue that connects with one of their main audiences.

full_record.file_path: 2024/ENTERTAINMENT LIONS FOR GAMING/GOLD/This Is Not A Game - Movistar (VML, Mexico City).webp

extracted.tagline: Film Craft / Production / Casting — We made reality look like a game so we became unignorable by gamers.
extracted.context: In Mexico, thousands of children are recruited by drug cartels each year. Over 81.4% of Mexico's gamer population feel the drug cartels, known as 'narcos', contact them through popular video games.
extracted.insight: Movistar Telecom is a brand that is committed to the digital environment. It cares about providing gamers with a safe space. 40% of Movistar Telecom business is in gaming, and the responsibility to address an issue that connects with one of their main audiences.
extracted.idea: 'This is Not a Game' was an unconventional project that demanded doing everything differently, even from the casting stage. Finding talent who could move and feel like game characters was not easy. The casting took place in schools, acting houses, gaming tournaments, and even video game fan pages. It was important to understand the world of video games to get into character immediately, so all of them were gamers.
execution: Level 1 Cultural Context: deep research into the recruitment of children by cartels through gaming
Level 2 Choose the Character: casting gamers from schools, acting houses and gaming tournaments
Level 3 Programming Talent: training the cast to move with game-like body control and mimic in-game movements
Film with cinematic, video-game-styled framing and tracked HUD overlays

inferred.one_liner: Reality filmed like a video game to reach recruited gamers.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Movistar reframes a film as a video-game-styled artefact to confront cartel recruitment of young gamers in Mexico, earning broad press pickup across regional and global outlets as a brand-purpose statement inside gaming culture.

visual.style_description: Cinematic film-still hero of an older man with a grey beard inside a car, framed with green video-game-style HUD overlays, targeting brackets and small data callouts; left side carries the bold white 'This Is Not A Game' title, right side stacks three numbered Film Craft text blocks, with a press logo strip running across the bottom.

idea.rationale: Filming reality in the visual grammar of a third-person video game — HUD overlays, targeting brackets, character-controlled body language — to confront cartel recruitment of Mexican gamers is a smart aesthetic hijack that lands the message inside the visual language of the target audience. The native-to-category check holds because the gaming look is not paint-on: casting was done in gaming tournaments and the performers are themselves gamers, which earns the form.

strategy.rationale: Movistar is Telefónica's Spanish-language telecom brand, and 40% of its Mexico business is gaming-connected, which makes player-safety a direct commercial concern rather than borrowed purpose. The context — '81.4% of Mexico's gamer population feel narcos contact them through video games' — is a specific, locally sourced data point that ties an enormous national problem (cartel recruitment of thousands of children per year) directly to the brand's distribution layer. The line from telecom-as-safe-digital-environment to anti-recruitment storytelling is coherent though it does tilt toward purpose.

execution.rationale: The three-level production system — cultural-context research, casting gamers from schools and gaming tournaments, then training the cast in game-like body mechanics — produces a film whose craft is the message: every shot is framed like a cutscene, with tracked HUD overlays and cinematic colour. Distribution leans on broad regional and global press pickup (CNN, El Pais, AdAge, The Drum, Contagious, MILENIO), which is right for a film-craft entry, though the campaign's in-game / streamer layer is not surfaced on the board, leaving the orchestration narrower than the Grand Prix benchmark.

impact.rationale: The board lists no campaign metrics — no view counts, no behaviour-change figures, no telecom KPI, no player-engagement metric of the kind the Gaming category typically requires. Impact rests entirely on the breadth of unpaid press across The Drum, AdAge, CNN, El Pais, MILENIO, Contagious and Campaign, which is a credible signal of earned conversation but is an entrant-reported logo wall rather than audited results. The conspicuous absence of any tune-in, viewership, brand-safety perception lift, or reduction-in-recruitment proxy is the principal soft spot.

why_it_won.verdict: Gold because the film-craft idea of shooting reality as a video game is genuinely native to gamer culture and addresses a specific Mexican harm Movistar's network is implicated in, even though the impact case rests on press logos rather than measured outcomes.

one_liner: The fantasy currency becomes its real Euro price.
search_doc:
The fantasy currency becomes its real Euro price.
Mechanisms: financial-literacy-tool, ai-currency-translator, browser-extension-as-media, consumer-advocacy-product
Idea: The In-Game Calculator by Sparkasse does something simple: it translates made-up in-game currencies back to real money. Available as a microsite, an AI-powered app that uses AI Technology to detect in-game currency from the smartphone camera (translates instantly to Euro), a browser extension covering major games, and a Twitch stream / YouTube tool that shows the Euro price on hover.
Insight: Real money is masked as fantasy currency in games, so players cannot see what they're actually spending; making that euro price visible at the point of purchase can restore informed decision-making.

full_record.file_path: 2024/ENTERTAINMENT LIONS FOR GAMING/BRONZE/Unhide The Price - Deutscher Sparkassen- Und Giroverband E.V. & S-Communication Services GMBH (Jung Von Matt AG, Hamburg).webp

extracted.tagline: Fighting the gaming industry's most diabolic pricing trick.
extracted.context: The gaming industry makes billions with in-game purchases. Every trick is used to squeeze more money out of the gamers. The most diabolic of all: the real prices of skins and other digital items are hidden behind fantasy currencies. A trick that has already driven gamers to financial ruin.
extracted.insight: Real money is masked as fantasy currency in games, so players cannot see what they're actually spending; making that euro price visible at the point of purchase can restore informed decision-making.
extracted.idea: The In-Game Calculator by Sparkasse does something simple: it translates made-up in-game currencies back to real money. Available as a microsite, an AI-powered app that uses AI Technology to detect in-game currency from the smartphone camera (translates instantly to Euro), a browser extension covering major games, and a Twitch stream / YouTube tool that shows the Euro price on hover.
execution: Microsite where players manually enter any fantasy currency and amount to get a Euro price
AI-powered smartphone app that detects in-game currency via the camera and converts instantly to Euros
Browser extension that translates prices on major gaming sites and stores
Twitch / YouTube extension that overlays the Euro price when the mouse hovers over an in-game item in a stream
press_coverage: HandOfBlood (Twitch streamer quote: 'This tool is super helpful, to make you aware: How much money is this perhaps not worth to me?', 1.2 Mio follower)

inferred.one_liner: The fantasy currency becomes its real Euro price.
inferred.qualitative_summary: A bank turns itself into a consumer-protection layer over the gaming economy, unmasking hidden in-game prices through a cross-platform calculator endorsed by major Twitch streamers.

visual.style_description: Dark cinematic hero of a heavily-armoured Apex Legends character on a black gradient with a small price label ('5,000 in-game currency = 49.90 EUR'); left column carries the editorial-style headline and body text in white; bottom strip shows the streamer testimonial alongside screenshots of the website, browser extension and mobile app.

idea.rationale: Turning a bank into a real-time consumer-protection layer over the gaming economy — translating in-game fantasy currencies back into Euros at the point of purchase — is a sharp product-as-message move that attacks a specific, named industry practice. The toolset (microsite, AI camera app, browser extension, Twitch overlay) gives the idea legs across the surfaces where the deception actually happens, rather than being a single piece of communication.

strategy.rationale: Sparkasse is Germany's largest savings-bank network (Deutscher Sparkassen- und Giroverband is its central association) whose long-standing public-good mission centres on financial literacy and consumer protection for ordinary German savers, so building a tool that exposes hidden in-game spending is precise mission-fit, not borrowed purpose. The insight — fantasy currencies mask real money and have driven gamers into financial ruin — is plain and observable, and the board ties it to a clear regulatory-adjacent harm that the Sparkasse brand has standing to address. The link from financial-literacy mission to in-game-currency calculator is one of the cleanest mission-fit arguments in the batch.

execution.rationale: Execution covers four real software surfaces — a web microsite, an AI smartphone app using camera-based currency recognition, a browser extension across major gaming sites, and a Twitch / YouTube overlay revealing the Euro price on hover — which is the right channel mix for intercepting purchase decisions across PC, mobile and streaming. Distribution on the board rests largely on a HandOfBlood endorsement (1.2M followers) rather than a structured streamer roster or platform partnership, which keeps the orchestration shallow for the toolset's scope.

impact.rationale: The board reports no campaign metrics at all — no app downloads, no extension installs, no microsite visits, no Twitch overlay activations, no behaviour-change measurement on in-game spending. The case rests on a single streamer quote from HandOfBlood (1.2M followers), which is a credible endorsement but is one entrant-selected testimonial rather than a measured outcome. The absence of any player-engagement metric — the one the Gaming category specifically requires — is the principal impact constraint.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the in-game calculator is a precisely mission-fit consumer-protection product for Sparkasse and the toolset is built across the right surfaces, but the case offers no measured player engagement or behaviour-change evidence to back the idea.
